texts.label: text
texts.orig: JGC Corporation (JGC) and NGK Insulators, Ltd. (NGK) have been developing the world ' s largest DDR-type zeolite membrane to meet the practical needs of CO2/hydrocarbon separation in the natural gas and CO2 Enhanced Oil Recovery (CO2-EOR) industries.
texts.text: JGC Corporation (JGC) and NGK Insulators, Ltd. (NGK) have been developing the world ' s largest DDR-type zeolite membrane to meet the practical needs of CO2/hydrocarbon separation in the natural gas and CO2 Enhanced Oil Recovery (CO2-EOR) industries.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: DDR-type zeolite has desirable nano-size pores for CO2/hydrocarbon separation. NGK has developed a thin, defect-free DDR-type zeolite membrane with a monolithic structure. This membrane is prepared onto the inner surface of a cylindrical monolithic alumina substrate having 30 channels with a diameter and length of 30 mm and 160 mm, respectively. CO2 permselectivity over CH4 of the monolithic membrane was investigated using the binary gas mixture of 61 -67 mol% CO2 and 33 -39 mol% CH4 at 44 -46 °C with up to 8.0 MPa G as the feed pressure. The CO2 permeability reached 1973 Barrer (6.6 × 10 -13 mol m m -2 s -1 Pa -1 ) while the separation factor of CO2/CH4 exceeded 140 at the transmembrane pressure di ff erence of 2.5 MPa. This CO2 permeability decreased to 1154 Barrer (3.9 × 10 -13 mol m m -2 s -1 Pa -1 ) at the transmembrane pressure di ff erence of 7.0 MPa; however, the separation factor of CO2/CH4 was still as high as 90. In order to understand the stability of the monolithic membrane, the change in the permeability of CO2 and CH4 with time was measured at 58 -60 °C with transmembrane pressure di ff erence of 7.4 -7.7 MPa using the binary gas mixture of 65 -71 mol% CO2 and 29 -35 mol% CH4 for 140 h. During the gas permeation test, the CO2 permeability was almost constant while CH4 permeability slightly decreased. As a result, the separation factor of CO2/CH4 was maintained at the initial value.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Based on the experimental results, we evaluated the potential applicability of the DDR-type zeolite membrane compared to the conventional polymer membrane for a CO2 recovery process in a CO2-EOR project where CO2 is often separated from the associated gas for re-use. We assumed that the associated gas contains 70 mol% CO2 and 30 mol% CH4 at a pressure of 4.0 MPa G while the speci fi cation of CO2 purity in the permeate gas was 95 mol % at a pressure of 0.3 MPa G. When this associated gas is treated using a single-stage membrane process, only 49% of CO2 was recovered by a membrane having a CO2/CH4 separation factor of 12.5 like a typical polymer membrane. On the other hand, membranes having CO2/CH4 separation factors of 80 -100 could recover 98 -99% of CO2 while more than 96 mol% purity of CH4 was obtained at the retentate side. This simulation study indicated that the highly selective membrane like the DDR-type zeolite membrane can recover not only high purity CO2 for CO2-EOR projects, but also make available CH4 for use or sale. This high purity CH4 brings additional bene fi t to the CO2-EOR operators.

texts.orig: receiving a lot of attention [2 -4]. Particularly, CO2 Enhanced Oil Recovery (CO2-EOR) is highly expected as the most important CCUS technology. In this process, CO2 is injected into an oil reservoir to increase oil production and recovery and a part of the injected CO2 is incidentally trapped in situ . The global CO2 storage potential during CO2-EOR operation over the next 50 years is estimated to be as much as 240 -360 Gt which is twice to three times the cumulative CO2 storage amount in the 2-Degree Celsius Scenario (2DS) [2]. At the same time, the incremental oil production resulting from CO2-EOR can reach up to 431 billion barrels.

In this process, CO2 is injected into an oil reservoir to increase oil production and recovery and a part of the injected CO2 is incidentally trapped in situ . The global CO2 storage potential during CO2-EOR operation over the next 50 years is estimated to be as much as 240 -360 Gt which is twice to three times the cumulative CO2 storage amount in the 2-Degree Celsius Scenario (2DS) [2]. At the same time, the incremental oil production resulting from CO2-EOR can reach up to 431 billion barrels.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Currently, more than 100 commercial CO2-EOR projects are in operation in the United States, and the daily oil production from these projects reaches 300,000 barrels. This production rate is expected to be doubled with increasing projects until 2020 [5]. Since CO2 purchase cost often accounts for most of the operating expense, low-cost CO2 is necessary for successful CO2-EOR projects [4]. However, the expanded use of costly anthropogenic CO2 is expected due to adapting to the future increase in CO2 demand and contributing to the reduction of CO2 accumulation [5 -7]. One of the practicable solutions is the re-use of CO2 in the production fl uid from CO2-EOR fi elds. It is known that part of the CO2 injected into an oil reservoir is partially produced with the oil and gas fl uid. This associated gas often contains high concentrations of CO2 which can be re-injected into the reservoir after proper separation and compression [4,7 -9]. According to the report from the National Energy Technology Laboratory, the typical purchase cost of CO2 from the market is 2 USD/Mcf whereas the cost of recycled CO2 is only one third of this on the assumption of an oil price of 70 USD/bbl [10].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For the CO2 recovery from an associated gas, a CO2 permeable polymer membrane is commonly used due to the favorable fl exibility [11,12]. The gas volume and CO2 content in the associated gas to be treated often unstably increases during the CO2-EOR operation, but the membrane system can be adjusted to cope with this by increasing the membrane area. However, other separation techniques like the amine based solvent process are required to be designed based on the maximum content of CO2 predicted by a reservoir simulation [12]. This may require undesirably high investment cost for the CO2 recovery system especially in the earlier stage of the CO2-EOR project. Although the polymer membrane system is widely adopted for this application, its insu ffi cient performance for CO2 permselectivity and durability are recognized as the technical issues. Because of the low CO2 selectivity over hydrocarbons and other gases, CO2 recovered by polymer membrane often contains CH4 and/or N2 in no small measure. This causes signi fi cant decrease in the incremental oil recovery by CO2-EOR due to the reduction of CO2 miscibility with oil [13,14]. Moreover, such impurities require extra power and decrease the e ffi ciency of the CO2 compression systems [7,14].

texts.orig: To provide a superior CO2 separation membrane, JGC Corporation (JGC) and NGK Insulators, Ltd. (NGK) have been developing the DDRtype zeolite membrane [15,16]. To date, NGK has been developing high quality inorganic membranes including commercially available microfi ltration and ultra fi ltration membrane fi lters [17]. Based on the accumulated know-how of porous ceramic product and fabrication technology for zeolite membrane, it has been the fi rst in the world to successfully prepare thin, defect-free DDR-type zeolite membrane onto the outer surface of a porous alumina substrate in 2004. This composite membrane showed 100 -220 of separation factor for CO2/CH4 with high CO2 permeability [18]. Since then, e ff orts have been made to increase the CO2 permselectivity and understand the permeation mechanism.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Van den Bergh et al. prepared 5 μ m thick zeolite membrane onto a porous alumina disc which showed very high separation factor of CO2/ CH4 up to 4000 -5000. Through the investigation on the e ff ect of temperature and pressure for CO2 and CH4 permeabilities, they concluded that the higher CO2 permeability can be attributed to its higher mobility and preferable adsorption a ffi nity with DDR-type zeolite [19]. Zhen et al. also observed the importance of the adsorption a ffi nity for the faster permeation through DDR-type zeolite membrane by H2 and CO2 permeation testing [20]. Kanezashi et al. examined a DDR-type zeolite membrane modi fi ed by the CVD method at elevated temperatures up to 500 °C. Since the e ff ect of the adsorption a ffi nity of CO2 with zeolite is reduced at higher temperatures, the CO2 permeability decreased with increasing temperature [21]. The gas transport mechanism through DDR-type zeolite was evaluated by van den Bergh et al. [22] and Krishna et al. [23] using relevant site model based on the MaxwellStefan framework and the combination of Grand Canonical Monte Carlo and molecular dynamics simulations, respectively. Krishna depicted that the selectively adsorbed CO2 onto DDR-type zeolite pore windows hinders the transportation of other molecules.

texts.orig: Membrane scale-up is another key advance. Recently, NGK has produced a large size of DDR-type zeolite membrane with a cylindrical monolithic structure [15]. This membrane with a diameter of 180 mm and a length of 1000 mm has 12 m 2 of surface area. To the best of our knowledge, this is the world ' s largest zeolite membrane element for gas separation. Its CO2 permeance surpasses 5 × 10 -7 mol m -2 s -1 Pa -1 under equimolar mixtures of CO2 and CH4 at a total feed pressure of 0.3 MPa G.

texts.orig: Although the understanding of the gas permeabilities at elevated pressure is essential for the practical use of the membrane, gas permeation testing at elevated pressure is limited. In this paper, we will introduce the results of gas permeation testing of the DDR-type zeolite monolithic membrane on a laboratory scale using a binary gas mixture of 61 -71 mol% CO2 and 29 -39 mol% CH4 at 44 -60 °C up to 8.0 MPa G as the feed pressure. In addition, the feasibility study of the DDR-type zeolite membrane when compared to the conventional polymer membrane for CO2 recovery process in a CO2-EOR project will be also introduced.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.1. Fabrication and features of the membrane
texts.text: 2.1. Fabrication and features of the membrane

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The photographs of laboratory scale and large-scale membrane elements are shown in the Fig. 1(a) and (b), respectively. The DDR-type zeolite membrane was formed onto the inner surface of channels in the alumina monolithic substrate via the hydrothermal synthesis method as described in Ref. [15]. To fabricate the high-quality DDR-type zeolite membrane onto each channel, there are several ingenuities: (i) Reducing defects of the surface of the alumina substrate, (ii) Homogeneous seeding onto the substrate, and (iii) Controlling the thickness of the zeolite membrane during the hydrothermal synthesis process. It is of note that the large-size monolithic membrane with a diameter of 180 mm and a length of 1000 mm has about 1600 channels, and the surface area reaches 12 m 2 which is 200 -300 times larger than conventional tubular type zeolite membranes of the same length [24]. From the viewpoint of mechanical engineering, this monolithic membrane has a great advantage in scale-up over conventional tubular membranes. Thus, this structure can meet the required surface area for a gas processing project with a smaller number of membrane elements. It can dramatically reduce the number of sealing and connecting parts for modularization of the membranes. In order to apply the monolithic membrane to high pressure CO2 separation such as natural gas processing and CO2 removal from associated gas, its pressure resistance is increased up to 8 MPa as the transmembrane pressure di ff erence. As shown in Fig. 2, we have successfully modularized the large size monolithic membrane in JGC ' s laboratory. A couple of monolithic elements can be installed into a module, and safely operated at elevated pressure. So far, despite the excellent CO2 permselectivity, zeolite membrane is not applied for the gas separation in industrial processes where a large-scale and robust membrane is required. Our high performance monolithic membrane with high pressure resistance should be promising for practical use in natural gas and CO2-EOR industries.

texts.orig: 2.2. Gas permeation experiments
texts.text: 2.2. Gas permeation experiments

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To understand the gas permeation properties of the DDR-type zeolite membrane, we conducted the gas permeation test using a laboratory size monolithic membrane which has 30 channels with a diameter and length of 30 mm and 160 mm, respectively shown in Fig. 1(a). The surface area of this membrane is 0.036 m 2 . Firstly, the monolithic membrane was fi xed in a stainless-steel vessel. Then, a binary gas mixture of 61 -67 mol% CO2 and 33 -39 mol% of CH4 was introduced from the cross-section of the monolithic membrane at 44 -46 °C, and the amount of gas that permeated was measured using a mass fl ow meter with transmembrane pressure di ff erences of 2.5 -7.0 MPa. At this time, the outer pressure of the monolithic membrane was kept at 0.6 -1.1 MPa G.

texts.orig: Fig. 4 shows that the gas permeation test for the DDR-type zeolite membrane at 58 -60 °C with transmembrane pressure di ff erence of 7.4 -7.7 MPa using the binary gas mixture of 65 -71 mol% CO2 and 29 -35 mol% CH4 for 140 h. During the test, the CO2 permeability was almost constant while CH4 permeability was slightly decreased. As a result, the separation factor of CO2/CH4 was maintained at the initial value. This indicated that the DDR-type zeolite membrane is defect-free and quite stable at the high pressure with high CO2 concentration condition. It is worthy of note that the durability of polymer membrane is insu ffi cient in such high pressure and high CO2 concentration condition. The CO2 selectivity of glassy polymer membrane is irreversibly decreased when it is exposed to high CO2 partial pressure condition. This phenomenon is well known as the polymer plasticization where the polymer structure is destructed by penetrated CO2 [28,29]. In the case of cellulose acetate membrane, the CO2-induced plasticization can be observed under only 1.0 -1.5 MPa of CO2 partial pressure [30]. This strongly indicates that the user of conventional polymer membrane is required to operate at lower pressure to avoid the plasticization.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.1. Role of CO2 separation process in CO2-EOR project
texts.text: 3.1. Role of CO2 separation process in CO2-EOR project

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As is well known, the key factor of enhanced oil recovery by CO2 is miscibility with oil. When CO2 is injected into an oil reservoir, CO2 and crude oil become gradually miscible by contacting each other. After reaching the completely miscible form, CO2 can displace the crude oil without residual oil microscopically [4]. This phenomenon occurs above a minimum pressure which is a characteristic value for each oil reservoir. Namely, the CO2-EOR operator should operate the project at above the minimum pressure to increase the oil production e ff ectively. Therefore, so-called minimum miscibility pressure (MMP) is experimentally investigated to determine the operating condition of the CO2EOR project. According to full or pilot scale CO2-EOR projects, 10 -20% of original oil in place (OOIP) can be recovered with miscible operation while less than half of it can be obtained with immiscible operation [31,32].
texts.text: As is well known, the key factor of enhanced oil recovery by CO2 is miscibility with oil. When CO2 is injected into an oil reservoir, CO2 and crude oil become gradually miscible by contacting each other. After reaching the completely miscible form, CO2 can displace the crude oil without residual oil microscopically [4]. This phenomenon occurs above a minimum pressure which is a characteristic value for each oil reservoir. Namely, the CO2-EOR operator should operate the project at above the minimum pressure to increase the oil production e ff ectively. Therefore, so-called minimum miscibility pressure (MMP) is experimentally investigated to determine the operating condition of the CO2EOR project. According to full or pilot scale CO2-EOR projects, 10 -20% of original oil in place (OOIP) can be recovered with miscible operation while less than half of it can be obtained with immiscible operation [31,32].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: However, it is known that the miscibility of CO2 with oil is in fl uenced by the purity of CO2. Zhang and co-workers investigated the impact of impurities on the thermodynamic MMP using Canadian reservoir fl uid. They revealed that only 10% impurities including equimolar CH4 and N2 increased MMP by 70% from 11.8 MPa to 20.5 MPa [13]. Wilkinson and co-workers also pointed out the signi fi cant increase in the MMP was observed when 75% or 85% purity of CO2 were injected into the West Texas reservoir fl uid instead of pure CO2 [7]. These experiments obviously indicate that the high purity of CO2 is necessary for a CO2-EOR project. Otherwise, the compression work will be increased to maintain the CO2-EOR operation while reaching miscible condition at above MMP of the impurity-containing CO2 and the crude oil. In fact, a major CO2 transporter, Kinder Morgan, set the minimum CO2 purity to 95% as one of the pipeline speci fi cations to guarantee the miscibility with petroleum [14].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In general, a CO2-EOR operator should maximize the re-use CO2 from the associated gas because it is even cheaper than CO2 purchased from the market. However, based on the above analysis, CO2 puri fi cation technology is required if the CO2 purity in the associated gas is not su ffi cient. Fig. 5 shows a typical fl ow of production fl uid in CO2-EOR with a CO2 recovery system. Thus, the production fl uid is introduced into a liquid/gas separation system which is composed of a couple of separators operated at di ff erent pressures. If CO2 concentrations in the separated gases from the gas/liquid separators are unfavorably low, a CO2 recovery system should be installed. Then, the recovered and/or further puri fi ed CO2 is fed to a compression system to be re-pressurized for re-injection. When a membrane based CO2 recovery system is applied, it is advantageous to feed CO2-contained associated gas from a high pressure separator because higher CO2 permeance can be obtained under larger transmembrane pressure di ff erence. Besides, if high purity CO2 can be recovered at some high pressure, it is able to decrease the compression energy for the re-injection.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: However, it should be noted that the range of CO2 concentrations in associated gas is very di ff erent, sometimes it is 40 -90% [11,33]. Therefore, conventional polymer membrane systems have been operating at lower pressure like 2.4 MPa G to avoid the polymer plasticization. In other words, the process design and the operating condition are restricted by the weak polymer membrane. Since our DDR-type zeolite membrane can be operated up to 8 MPa G without damage by CO2, it contributes to optimize the CO2 recovery system with recovering higher purity CO2 under elevated operating pressure.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig. 7(a) and (b) show the e ff ect of the separation factor of CO2/CH4 for the treated gases. To recover more than 95 mol% purity of CO2, CO2 recovery was limited to as low as 49% when a less selective membrane having 12.5 of CO2/CH4 separation factor like a typical polymer membrane was used. In addition, the CH4 concentration in the retentate was only 44 mol%. Moreover, the deterioration of membrane performance based on the CO2-induced plasticization can occur at such high CO2 conditions [25,26]. On the other hand, membranes having 80 -100 of CO2/CH4 separation factor could recover 98 -99% of CO2 while more than 96 mol% purity of CH4 was obtained at the retentate side. According to a typical pipeline speci fi cation for natural gas, CO2 content should be reduced to 2 -4 mol% [34]. This simulation study indicates that the high selective membrane like the DDR-type zeolite membrane can provide high purity CO2 for EOR projects as well as the additional revenue by selling natural gas for CO2-EOR operator.
